page: Vultr voc-m-24c-192gb-1200s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server featuring an AMD EPYC processor with 24 vCPUs and 12 physical cores. Utilizing the x86_64 architecture, this instance operates at a base clock speed of 2.0 GHz and supports multithreading with two threads per core. It is configured with 192.0 GB of system memory, yielding 8.0 GB of RAM per core, and includes a bundled 1200 GB NVMe SSD for local storage.

In benchmark evaluations, the server demonstrates strong CPU performance, ranking in the top 25% for both single-core and multi-core stress-ng tests, as well as the PassMark CPU physics test. Memory bandwidth and data processing metrics fall within the average range, with read-only large block memory bandwidth measured at 141.36 GB/sec and write bandwidth at 71.93 GB/sec. Database operations and Redis SET performance also align with average tier expectations, executing 42.05 million operations per second.

The instance utilizes a shared CPU allocation model, which presents a potential performance variability tradeoff compared to dedicated CPU instances. However, the high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 1:8 and the inclusion of fast local NVMe storage provide a cost-efficient profile for memory-intensive workloads that do not require continuous, dedicated CPU execution.

This hardware configuration is suited for memory-intensive applications, database hosting, caching layers such as Redis, and moderate data processing tasks. It is also capable of handling basic large language model inference, achieving 17.66 tokens per second for text generation on a medium 7B model.
description: The Vultr voc-m-24c-192gb-1200s-amd is an Optimized Cloud Compute server powered by an AMD EPYC processor with 24 vCPUs and 12 physical cores on the x86_64 architecture. It features a high memory-to-vCPU ratio with 192.0 GB of RAM (8.0 GB per core) and includes a 1200 GB NVMe SSD for local storage. Operating with a shared CPU allocation at 2.0 GHz, the server delivers strong performance in stress-ng single-core and multi-core benchmarks, placing in the top 25%. Memory bandwidth and database operations perform in the average tier, with Redis SET operations reaching 42.05 million operations per second. This configuration offers a cost-effective balance of high memory density and local storage, making it suitable for memory-optimized workloads, database hosting, caching layers, and moderate data processing tasks.
